frozen_components: Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (used for abbreviation reduction and synthetic data generation, frozen during training)
architecture.summary: DR.EHR is a series of dense retrieval models (110M and 7B parameters) trained via a two-stage pipeline on MIMIC-IV discharge summaries. Stage I injects medical knowledge from the BIOS biomedical knowledge graph by identifying entity mentions, reducing abbreviations via LLM, and enriching positive samples with synonyms, hypernyms, and related entities. Stage II fine-tunes using LLM-generated synthetic training data (diseases, procedures, drugs) to optimize for entity retrieval. Both stages use contrastive learning with Multi-Similarity Loss and in-batch negatives. The small variant initializes from bge-base-en-v1.5 (BERT-based, [CLS] pooling); the large variant initializes from NV-Embed-v2 (Mistral decoder, last-token pooling). Text representations are compared via cosine similarity.

biblio.title: DR.EHR: Dense Retrieval for Electronic Health Record with Knowledge Injection and Synthetic Data

claims.statement: DR.EHR-small with 110M parameters outperforms all existing dense retrievers, achieving MAP of 89.12 on Single-Patient Retrieval compared to NV-Embed-v2's 80.21
DR.EHR-large with 7B parameters achieves Recall@100 of 67.04 on Multi-Patient Retrieval compared to NV-Embed-v2's 51.54
DR.EHR-small achieves 97.34 average score on string matching compared to bge-base-en-v1.5's 86.75
DR.EHR-small achieves 83.37 average score on abbreviation matching compared to bge-base-en-v1.5's 57.15 (26% improvement)
Stage I knowledge injection training improves Recall@100 on Multi-Patient Retrieval by approximately 5% across semantic match types when compared to w/o stage I baseline
Manual evaluation of 50 randomly sampled note chunks containing 831 LLM-generated entities revealed that 709 entities (85%) are clinically validated
Training pipeline generates over 5.8M note chunks from 332k MIMIC-IV discharge summaries, with Stage I producing 802M total positive samples and Stage II producing 86M positive samples
Models demonstrate strong generalizability to natural language questions including complex ones with multiple entities, despite training exclusively on single-entity queries
